---
title: "Food Fraud Analysis Practicals"
subtitle: "FBMFOR — MSc Food Technology and Quality Assurance"
author: "Department of Food and Nutritional Sciences, University of Reading"
date: last-modified
---

## Contents

- [Chemometric Analysis](#chemometric-analysis)
  - [What You Can Do](#what-you-can-do)
  - [Data Format](#data-format)
  - [How to Use](#how-to-use)
  - [Tips for Your Report](#tips-for-your-report)
- [XRD Starch Analysis](#xrd-starch-analysis)
  - [What You Will Do](#what-you-will-do)
  - [Polymorph Background](#polymorph-background)
- [NMR Honey Analysis](#nmr-honey-analysis)
  - [What You Will Do](#what-you-will-do-1)
  - [Key Spectral Regions](#key-spectral-regions)
- [FTIR Honey Analysis](#ftir-honey-analysis)
  - [What You Will Do](#what-you-will-do-2)
  - [Fingerprint Region](#fingerprint-region)
- [IRMS Olive Oil Origin](#irms-olive-oil-origin)
  - [What You Will Do](#what-you-will-do-3)
  - [Isotope Systems](#isotope-systems)
- [SEM Food Microscopy](#sem-food-microscopy)
  - [What You Will Do](#what-you-will-do-4)
  - [Image Statistics](#image-statistics)
- [References](#references)

---

## Chemometric Analysis

```{=html}
<div class="launch-buttons">
  <a href="https://colab.research.google.com/github/ggkuhnle/fbmfor-foodfraud/blob/main/notebooks/FBMFOR_Chemometrics_Analysis.ipynb" target="_blank" class="btn-colab">
    <img src="https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg" alt="Open In Colab">
    Google Colab
  </a>
  <a href="https://mybinder.org/v2/gh/ggkuhnle/fbmfor-foodfraud/main?filepath=notebooks/FBMFOR_Chemometrics_Analysis.ipynb" target="_blank" class="btn-binder">
    <img src="https://mybinder.org/badge_logo.svg" alt="Open in Binder">
    Binder (no login required)
  </a>
</div>
```

### About This Notebook

This interactive notebook provides a complete chemometric analysis pipeline for spectroscopic data used in food fraud detection. It is designed for postgraduate students on the FBMFOR module to analyse NMR and FTIR data from their laboratory practicals.

The notebook runs entirely in **Google Colab** — no installation required. Simply click the badge above.

### What You Can Do

#### Unsupervised Methods

**Principal Component Analysis (PCA)**
: Explore the natural structure of your spectral data. PCA reveals groupings, outliers, and the spectral regions driving variation — all without using class labels. The notebook provides interactive scores plots with 95% confidence ellipses, loadings plots, scree plots, and Hotelling's T² outlier detection.

**Hierarchical Cluster Analysis (HCA)**
: Visualise sample similarity as a dendrogram. Choose from different distance metrics and linkage methods to assess how well your samples cluster by class.

#### Supervised Methods

**PLS-DA (Partial Least Squares Discriminant Analysis)**
: Classify samples using their spectral fingerprints. The notebook automatically optimises the number of latent variables via cross-validation and reports confusion matrices, classification accuracy, and VIP scores to identify the most discriminating spectral regions.

**OPLS-DA (Orthogonal PLS-DA)**
: Separate predictive variation from orthogonal (irrelevant) variation for cleaner class separation. Includes S-plots for biomarker identification and permutation testing for model validation.

#### Preprocessing

All standard spectral preprocessing methods are available with adjustable parameters:

| Method | Purpose | Typical Use |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| ALS baseline correction | Remove baseline drift | FTIR |
| SNV normalisation | Remove scatter effects | FTIR, NIR |
| Area normalisation | Equalise total intensity | NMR |
| Min-max scaling | Scale to [0, 1] | General |
| Savitzky-Golay derivatives | Resolve overlapping peaks, remove baseline | FTIR, NIR |

### Data Format

The notebook accepts **CSV files** with a simple structure:

**Spectral data** — one row per sample, one column per spectral variable (wavenumber or chemical shift). The first column must be `Sample_ID`.

**Metadata** — a lookup table mapping `Sample_ID` to `Class` labels (e.g., "EVOO", "Adulterated").

A **demo dataset** (simulated olive oil FTIR with three classes: EVOO, Refined, Adulterated) is built into the notebook so you can explore the full pipeline immediately.

### How to Use

1. **Open the notebook** by clicking the Colab badge at the top of this section
2. **Run Section 1** (Setup) to load all required libraries
3. **Run Section 2** (Demo Dataset) to generate the example data, *or* go to Section 3 to upload your own CSV files
4. **Work through each section** sequentially — modify the clearly labelled parameter blocks (e.g., `NORMALISATION = "snv"`, `PC_X = 1`) and re-run the cell to see the effect
5. **Export results** (Section 10) to download PCA scores, loadings, and VIP scores as CSV files for your report

### Tips for Your Report

- **Always show preprocessing** — include before/after spectra plots and justify your choices
- **PCA is exploratory** — report the explained variance and describe what you see in the scores plot, but remember it does not use class information
- **Cross-validate supervised models** — never report calibration accuracy alone; always use the cross-validated results
- **VIP scores and S-plots** — use these to link your statistical results back to specific chemical features (e.g., particular absorption bands)
- **Permutation testing** — a p-value < 0.05 confirms your OPLS-DA model is detecting genuine class differences, not overfitting

---

## XRD Starch Analysis

```{=html}
<div class="launch-buttons">
  <a href="https://colab.research.google.com/github/ggkuhnle/fbmfor-foodfraud/blob/main/notebooks/XRD_Starch_Analysis.ipynb" target="_blank" class="btn-colab">
    <img src="https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg" alt="Open In Colab">
    Google Colab
  </a>
  <a href="https://mybinder.org/v2/gh/ggkuhnle/fbmfor-foodfraud/main?filepath=notebooks/XRD_Starch_Analysis.ipynb" target="_blank" class="btn-binder">
    <img src="https://mybinder.org/badge_logo.svg" alt="Open in Binder">
    Binder (no login required)
  </a>
</div>
```

**Data:** [Download xrd.csv](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ggkuhnle/fbmfor-foodfraud/main/data/xrd.csv)

### About This Practical

Starch is an important ingredient for many foods - and starch from different foods have different properties. Because different botanical sources produce starches with distinct crystal structures (polymorphs), X-ray diffraction (XRD) provides a rapid fingerprint that can reveal the botanical origin of a starch sample — and detect substitution.

This practical uses real diffractogram data from five starch types: Corn, Wheat, Potato, Tapioca, and an **Unknown** sample. Your task is to identify the Unknown using peak pattern matching.

The notebook loads data directly from GitHub — no file upload needed in Colab.

### What You Will Do

**Waterfall plot** — visualise all five diffractograms offset for comparison and analyse the data


### Polymorph Background

| Polymorph | Starches | Key peaks (2θ, Cu Kα) |
|-----------|----------|----------------------|
| **A-type** (cereal) | Wheat, Corn | 15.0°, 17.0°, 17.9°, 22.9° |
| **B-type** (tuber/root) | Potato, Tapioca | 5.6°, 15.1°, 17.2°, 19.7°, 22.2°, 24.0°, 26.4° |

The low-angle peak at ~5.6° is the most diagnostic feature of B-type starches — it is absent in A-type patterns.

---

## NMR Honey Analysis

```{=html}
<div class="launch-buttons">
  <a href="https://colab.research.google.com/github/ggkuhnle/fbmfor-foodfraud/blob/main/notebooks/NMR_Honey_Analysis.ipynb" target="_blank" class="btn-colab">
    <img src="https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg" alt="Open In Colab">
    Google Colab
  </a>
  <a href="https://mybinder.org/v2/gh/ggkuhnle/fbmfor-foodfraud/main?filepath=notebooks/NMR_Honey_Analysis.ipynb" target="_blank" class="btn-binder">
    <img src="https://mybinder.org/badge_logo.svg" alt="Open in Binder">
    Binder (no login required)
  </a>
</div>
```

### About This Practical

¹H NMR spectroscopy provides a detailed chemical fingerprint of honey composition, capturing the relative abundance of sugars, organic acids, amino acids, and other small molecules. Because different honey types and adulterants have characteristic sugar profiles, NMR is a powerful tool for authentication and fraud detection.

This notebook reads Bruker TopSpin processed spectra (binary `1r` files) directly — no conversion needed. It displays the reference sugar spectra alongside the unknown honey samples, then performs PCA on binned spectral data to reveal chemical differences between samples.

The notebook runs in **Google Colab** and loads data directly from GitHub — no file upload required. Simply click the badge above and run all cells.

### What You Will Do

**Reference spectra** — waterfall plot of pure sugar standards (sucrose, fructose, glucose, and others) for comparison.

**Sample spectra** — waterfall plot of the honey samples: Greek honey, Golden Syrup, Slovak honey, Honey in Ramekin, and Liquid honey.

**Glucose/Fructose ratio** — semi-quantitative estimation of the fructose-to-glucose ratio using NMR peak integrals. Glucose is measured from its two anomeric windows (α-Glc at ~5.22 ppm and β-Glc at ~4.63 ppm); fructose from two narrow windows centred at ~4.10 ppm and ~4.00 ppm. Typical honey has a Fru/Glc ratio greater than 1, but the value should be interpreted as a diagnostic estimate rather than an absolute concentration ratio.

**Spectral binning** — spectra are divided into 0.04 ppm bins over 0.5–9.0 ppm; the HDO solvent peak (4.5–5.0 ppm) is excluded before chemometric analysis.

**PCA** — scores plot (PC1 vs PC2) and loadings plot using the honey samples only, identifying which spectral regions drive the separation between sample types.


---

## FTIR Honey Analysis

```{=html}
<div class="launch-buttons">
  <a href="https://colab.research.google.com/github/ggkuhnle/fbmfor-foodfraud/blob/main/notebooks/FTIR_Honey_Analysis.ipynb" target="_blank" class="btn-colab">
    <img src="https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg" alt="Open In Colab">
    Google Colab
  </a>
  <a href="https://mybinder.org/v2/gh/ggkuhnle/fbmfor-foodfraud/main?filepath=notebooks/FTIR_Honey_Analysis.ipynb" target="_blank" class="btn-binder">
    <img src="https://mybinder.org/badge_logo.svg" alt="Open in Binder">
    Binder (no login required)
  </a>
</div>
```

### About This Practical

ATR-FTIR spectroscopy produces a detailed chemical fingerprint of honey composition in minutes. This notebook reads Perkin-Elmer Spectrum CSV exports from `data/ftir/`, converts %T to absorbance, and analyses the carbohydrate fingerprint region (1800–650 cm⁻¹).

The spectra are already baseline-corrected by the instrument software. Sample IDs are derived automatically from filenames.

### What You Will Do {#what-you-will-do-2}

**Overlay and waterfall plots** — visualise all honey spectra simultaneously and offset for individual comparison, with diagnostic band annotations (H₂O bend at 1640 cm⁻¹, fructose C-OH at 1095/1053 cm⁻¹, glucose C-OH at 1032 cm⁻¹, ring vibrations at 892 cm⁻¹).

**Fingerprint region zoom** — focus on 1800–650 cm⁻¹ where the most diagnostic sugar bands appear.

**PCA** — scores plot, loadings plot, and biplot using the fingerprint region, revealing which wavenumber channels drive differences between honey samples.

### Fingerprint Region

| Band (cm⁻¹) | Assignment |
|-------------|-----------|
| 1640 | H₂O bending |
| 1095 | C-OH stretch (fructose) |
| 1053 | C-OH stretch (fructose) |
| 1032 | C-OH stretch (glucose) |
| 892 | Ring vibrations (sugars) |

---

## IRMS Olive Oil Origin

```{=html}
<div class="launch-buttons">
  <a href="https://colab.research.google.com/github/ggkuhnle/fbmfor-foodfraud/blob/main/notebooks/IRMS_Olive_Oil_Origin.ipynb" target="_blank" class="btn-colab">
    <img src="https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg" alt="Open In Colab">
    Google Colab
  </a>
  <a href="https://mybinder.org/v2/gh/ggkuhnle/fbmfor-foodfraud/main?filepath=notebooks/IRMS_Olive_Oil_Origin.ipynb" target="_blank" class="btn-binder">
    <img src="https://mybinder.org/badge_logo.svg" alt="Open in Binder">
    Binder (no login required)
  </a>
</div>
```

### About This Practical

Stable isotope ratio mass spectrometry (IRMS) measures the natural abundance of heavy isotopes in food. Because isotope ratios reflect geography (precipitation, temperature) and agricultural practice (fertilisation, irrigation), IRMS is widely used by food standards agencies to verify geographic origin claims and detect adulteration.

This notebook reads `data/irms/irms.csv` (long format: Sample / delta / IR), pivots it to wide format, and compares the olive oil samples against published reference regions for Mediterranean-origin extra-virgin olive oils.

### What You Will Do {#what-you-will-do-3}

**δ²H vs δ¹⁸O origin map** — plot each sample against published ±1 SD reference ellipses for Italy, Spain, Greece, and Tunisia. Both hydrogen and oxygen isotopes track the meteoric water at the growing site; Italian oils cluster at lower δ¹⁸O than other Mediterranean origins.

**δ¹³C strip plot** — C3 oils (olive, sunflower, rapeseed; −32 to −22‰) are clearly separated from C4 adulterants (corn oil; −16 to −9‰). Even 5–10% corn oil addition is detectable.

**Nearest-centroid assignment** — normalised Euclidean distance in δ²H/δ¹⁸O space assigns each sample to the closest reference region, with flagging of ambiguous assignments.

### Isotope Systems

| Isotope ratio | Reference scale | Key application |
|---------------|----------------|----------------|
| δ²H (‰) | VSMOW | Geographic origin (precipitation signal) |
| δ¹⁸O (‰) | VSMOW | Geographic origin (primary discriminant for Italy) |
| δ¹³C (‰) | VPDB | C3/C4 adulteration detection |

> Torres-Cobos et al. (2025) *Food Chem.* doi:10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.141998

---

## SEM Food Microscopy

```{=html}
<div class="launch-buttons">
  <a href="https://colab.research.google.com/github/ggkuhnle/fbmfor-foodfraud/blob/main/notebooks/SEM_Food_Microscopy.ipynb" target="_blank" class="btn-colab">
    <img src="https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg" alt="Open In Colab">
    Google Colab
  </a>
  <a href="https://mybinder.org/v2/gh/ggkuhnle/fbmfor-foodfraud/main?filepath=notebooks/SEM_Food_Microscopy.ipynb" target="_blank" class="btn-binder">
    <img src="https://mybinder.org/badge_logo.svg" alt="Open in Binder">
    Binder (no login required)
  </a>
</div>
```

### About This Practical

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) reveals food microstructure at sub-micron resolution, making it possible to identify individual starch granules, pollen grains, or adulterant particles that are indistinguishable by bulk chemical methods. This notebook loads 16-bit greyscale TIFF images from `data/sem/`, organises them by sample code, and extracts quantitative texture statistics.

Eight sample groups are included (CMA, CMB, CoA, CoB, GM, GMS, NB, sand), each with four fields of view.

### What You Will Do {#what-you-will-do-4}

**Contact-sheet gallery** — display all images grouped by sample with a shared greyscale scale per group, so brightness differences between fields of view are immediately apparent.

**Image statistics table** — per-image mean intensity, standard deviation, coefficient of variation (CV), and pixel dimensions.

**CV bar chart** — compare texture heterogeneity across all sample groups. Sand shows the highest CV (most heterogeneous surface), while processed starches tend to be more uniform.

**Intensity histograms** — pixel-value distributions reveal whether each sample is uniform (narrow, symmetric peak) or heterogeneous (broad or bimodal distribution).

### Image Statistics

| Statistic | Meaning |
|-----------|---------|
| Mean intensity | Overall brightness — lower = denser / darker material |
| SD | Standard deviation — proxy for local contrast |
| CV (%) | SD / mean × 100 — normalised texture, comparable across brightnesses |

---

## References


- Dome K, Podgorbunskikh E, Bychkov A, Lomovsky O. Changes in the Crystallinity Degree of Starch Having Different Types of Crystal Structure after Mechanical Pretreatment. *Polymers (Basel)*. 2020 Mar 12;12(3):641. [doi:10.3390/polym12030641](https://doi.org/10.3390/polym12030641). PMID: 32178224; PMCID: PMC7183072.

- Torres-Cobos B et al. Discrimination of Italian and non-Italian virgin olive oils by stable isotope ratio analysis. *Food Chemistry*. 2025. [doi:10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.141998](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.141998)

- Bontempo L et al. Multi-element (H, C, N, O, S) stable isotope ratio analysis for determining the geographical origin of Italian extra-virgin olive oils. *Food Control*. 2019;98:482–489. [doi:10.1016/j.foodcont.2018.11.038](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodcont.2018.11.038)

- Szaniawska A et al. Data-Driven FTIR Spectroscopy for the Discrimination of Nectars. *Molecules*. 2025;30(20):4083. [doi:10.3390/molecules30204083](https://doi.org/10.3390/molecules30204083)

---

**Contact:** Gunter Kuhnle — g.g.kuhnle@reading.ac.uk  
Department of Food and Nutritional Sciences, University of Reading
